patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hello , how are you ?"] i'm good , thank you .

[Clinician asked: "so tell me what brings you in today ."] recently i tried to donate blood , around december i think , and they told me i was anemic , which is something i've been dealing with for a while , so it's not the first time i've been told i'm anemic .

[Clinician asked: "or how have you been feeling in general with this ?"] not great . i have been feeling fatigued often during the day , and even feverish with chills at times . when i try to be active i like i ca n't catch my breath and i feel like i'm wheezing . i've had some headaches too , which is not like me .

[Clinician asked: "okay . are there any other symptoms ?"] i've been noting some chilling sensations . i also get cold so easily . it's annoying . i feel like i have to really bundle up . i do n't know if this is related but my anxiety and depression feel like it has been getting worse lately . i feel like a mess .

[Clinician asked: "sounds like you're not feeling great , obviously . and i'm glad you came to see us . um , we're certainly going to try to figure this out and figure out what's going on , uh , but it sounds like you've been dealing with this anemia for a long time ?"] yeah , i've been anemic since i was 13 years old .

[Clinician asked: "right . so why do your doctors think you're anemic ? do you have a history of heavy periods ?"] well i did have heavy periods until i had a hysterectomy in 1996 . but no , they have not told me why they think i'm anemic , which is frustrating honestly .

[Clinician asked: "yeah . i can imagine that is . um , let's see if we can help though . since you had your hysterectomy your periods , of course , are no longer the issue . um , when was your last colonoscopy ?"] about five to six years ago .
[Clinician asked: "and was it relatively a normal exam ? did you have any polyps ?"] no . they said they'd see me in 10 years .
[Clinician asked: "well that's good news ."] yeah , i agree .
[Clinician asked: "um , do you have a pacemaker or defibrillator , or have sleep apnea , or use oxygen at night ?"] no .
[Clinician asked: "all right . do you ever drink alcohol ?"] yeah , but only once or twice a year .
[Clinician asked: "okay . are you taking any supplements such as iron or vitamin b12 ?"] i already started taking my iron pills which i have not taken in about a year .
[Clinician asked: "all right . and what are you taking ?"] i'm taking 25 milligram tablets , twice daily .
[Clinician asked: "okay , and that's the , the ferrous sulfate ?"] yeah , that's it . i take one in the morning and one in the evening .
[Clinician asked: "okay . anything else ?"] yeah , i take vitamin b12 , just the over the counter stuff .
[Clinician asked: "okay , very good . all right , well let's go ahead and take a look and see what's going on ."] sounds good . thank you .
[Clinician asked: "of course . you'll hear me , uh , talk through your exam so that i get all the information documented ."] okay .
[Clinician asked: "all right . so use my general physical exam template . i will start by listening to your heart and lungs ."] okay .
[Clinician asked: "all right . next , i'd like you to lay back so i can examine your abdomen ."] okay .
[Clinician asked: "is there any tenderness where i'm pressing ?"] no .

[Clinician asked: "okay . you can sit up . so your physical exam is normal without any significant findings . all right ms. cooper , often when we initially see anemia and your host of symptoms , we suspect internal bleeding ."] is that why they want me to have another upper endoscopy ?
[Clinician asked: "actually it would be an upper endoscopy and a colonoscopy , but yes , likely that's the reason why ."] lovely .

[Clinician asked: "yeah . unfortunately our cameras do not meet all the way in the middle , so if those tests back , come , if those tests come back fine , then we'll have you swallow a pill camera to take pictures as it moves through your , uh , system ."] okay .

[Clinician asked: "we may not need to , but it's just the first thing we can do to make sure that you're not losing blood . um , the second thing we can do is have you see a hematologist . they will tell us if you need to give any , to give you any intravenous iron , or maybe something to help your body store the iron"] all right .
[Clinician asked: "so let's go ahead , get your upper endoscopy and colonoscopy scheduled ."] okay .
[Clinician asked: "um , have you ever had any issue with sedation in the past ?"] no , i was just sleepy afterwards .

[Clinician asked: "okay . well we will give you a bowel prep to clean out your bowels ahead of time . um , if we do these tests and they are normal , like i said , then we will consider that capsule endoscopy ."] okay . sounds like a plan .

[Clinician asked: "all right . so after that you'll be all done and we will send you to the hematologist . additionally , i'm going to need you to start taking your iron pills with orange juice . uh , the vitamin c will help you absorb the iron better . do this for about 8-12 weeks , uh , and then we can reassess your"] okay , that sounds great .

[Clinician asked: "all right . well i think we have our plan . on your way out , stop by and schedule your upper endoscopy and c- colonoscopy . uh , we will send a referral to dr. flores who is is the hematologist , so schedule that appointment . um , here are your instructions for the pre- uh , the bowel prep . uh , "] thank you .
[Clinician asked: "you're welcome . have a great day , have a great day ms. cooper ."] you too .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Iron deficiency anemia.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mrs. Christina Cooper is a 65-year-old female who presents with a long-history of iron deficiency anemia.

The patient reports she has been living with anemia since she was 13 years old. She attempted to donate blood on 12/2019 but was unable to due to her anemia. She has a history of heavy menstruation and a hysterectomy in 1996. She completed a colonoscopy 5-6 years ago with nonsignificant findings. She denies having a pacemaker, defibrillator, sleep apnea, or using oxygen.

Mrs. Cooper reports a myriad of symptoms, stating she feels fatigued often during the day, feverish with chills at times, and during activity feels she cannot catch her breath and some wheezing is present. She has been having some headaches which is uncommon for her. Additionally, she is noticing tingling sensations, cold sensitivity, and her anxiety and depression have worsened.

PAST HISTORY

Surgical
Hysterectomy, 1996.

Procedures
Colonoscopy 5-6 years ago.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Drink’s alcohol 1-2 times per year.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Ferrous Sulfate 25 mg tablet twice per day (1 in the morning, 1 in the evening), Vitamin B12 OTC.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Gastrointestinal
Abdomen non-tender.

ASSESSMENT

• Iron deficiency anemia

Mrs. Cooper is a 65-year-old female who was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia in 12/2019 and was unable to donate blood. Her follow-up blood work in 01/2020 revealed a low hemoglobin, stable hematocrit, and normal iron labs, although, ferritin was low. She has been taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg by mouth.

PLAN

I have asked her to continue each ferrous sulfate dose with vitamin C found in orange juice for the next 12 weeks then recheck CBC, iron ferritin, B-12, and folate. A referral was sent for her to see a hematologist. We will plan for EGD and colonoscopy to assess for potential sources of anemia or GI bleed. If this is inconclusive, capsule endoscopy can be considered.

INSTRUCTIONS

Schedule upper endoscopy, colonoscopy and appointment with hematologist. Return to the clinic if symptoms worsen.

naive.response_text:
Dear [Patient's Name],

I understand how frustrating and exhausting it must be to deal with anemia for so long, especially when it's affecting your daily life and making you feel like a mess. I want you to know that we're here to figure this out together. The tests we're planning, like the upper endoscopy and colonoscopy, are important steps to ensure we're addressing any underlying issues. 

In the meantime, taking your iron supplements with orange juice can help improve absorption, and I encourage you to stick with it. We'll also be scheduling an appointment with a hematologist to explore any additional treatments, like intravenous iron, that might be helpful. Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or concerns in the meantime. You're doing the right thing by taking care of yourself, and we're here to support you every step of the way.

Take care, and let's get through this together.

Warm regards,  
[Your Name]

strong_prompt.response_text:
Thank you for sharing all of this with me, Ms. Cooper. It sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed and frustrated, especially after dealing with anemia for so long and not having clear answers about why it's happening. I can understand how that would make you feel like a "mess," and I want to acknowledge how strong you are for continuing to manage this despite the challenges.

Given your symptoms—like fatigue, shortness of breath, and feeling cold all the time—it’s important we check for any internal bleeding, which can often cause these kinds of symptoms. That’s why we’re planning the upper endoscopy and colonoscopy. These tests will help us rule out any hidden bleeding and give us a clearer picture of what’s going on. If those come back normal, we might consider the capsule endoscopy as the next step.

In the meantime, I want to make sure you’re getting the most out of your iron supplements. Taking them with orange juice can really help with absorption, so I’ll include that in your instructions. We’ll schedule a follow-up in 8-12 weeks to reassess your symptoms and see how things are improving. Before you leave, could you summarize what we’ve discussed today? That way, I can make sure everything makes sense to you.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in a few weeks. In the meantime, if you notice any new symptoms or if your current symptoms worsen, please call us right away.

full.response_text:
I can hear how frustrating this has been for you — you mentioned it's frustrating honestly. I want to make sure I understand your concerns before diving into any clinical details.

Given your history of anemia since age 13 and the interventions you've tried, we'll focus on ruling out specific causes. This includes checking for iron deficiency, vitamin B12 levels, and possibly other deficiencies.

Your fatigue, especially with symptoms like feverish chills and shortness of breath, suggests we should explore underlying causes. We'll start with basic bloodwork and possibly imaging to rule out infections or other issues.

We'll schedule a blood test to check your iron and vitamin B12 levels, as well as a complete blood count to assess your anemia.

Based on the results, we may recommend further tests, such as a colonoscopy, given your history.

We'll also monitor your fatigue and see if there are any specific triggers or patterns we can identify together.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to follow up in two weeks to review the test results. In the meantime, if your symptoms worsen or you develop new ones like severe fatigue or dizziness, please call us right away.
